query: management of thyroid cancer in pregnancy

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Disease and Pregnancy -- Medical Oncology -- Thyroid Nodules and Thyroid Cancer During Pregnancy
retrieved_docs.content: The prevalence of thyroid nodules during pregnancy varies between 3% and 21%. [42] The evaluation and management of thyroid nodules during pregnancy are similar to those with non-pregnant patients. A thyroid ultrasound should be performed, and indications for fine needle aspiration (FNA) are similar to those in non-pregnant patients. FNA is safe to be performed during pregnancy. All women with thyroid nodules should have a TSH level measured to rule out follicular adenoma, ie, toxic adenoma. Results from multiple studies confirm that carcinoma of the thyroid gland is the second most frequent pregnancy-associated cancer, behind carcinoma of the breast. Registry studies suggest that thyroid cancer is present in between 14 and 27 per 100,000 mothers giving birth. [25] [43]

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Disease and Pregnancy -- Medical Oncology -- Thyroid Nodules and Thyroid Cancer During Pregnancy
retrieved_docs.content: The prevalence of thyroid cancer varies widely due to differences in study design and patient population. The prognosis of differentiated thyroid cancer is not largely influenced by pregnancy. [44] Given the slow growth of differentiated thyroid cancer, definitive management (thyroidectomy) can usually be delayed up until after delivery. The biopsy-proven thyroid cancer nodules should be monitored with a thyroid ultrasound every trimester. In rare circumstances, such as rapidly growing thyroid nodules or in the presence of metastases, surgery is indicated during pregnancy. In such cases, the second trimester is the safest period for performing the surgery. [18] [45]

retrieved_docs.title: Radioactive Iodine Therapy for Thyroid Malignancies -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Using checklists can help explain all aspects of therapy to the patient, improve clinical outcomes, and reduce radiation exposure to the public. Patients should maintain a distance of at least 3 feet from others, especially pregnant women and children, for the first few days after radioactive iodine therapy. In addition, patients should be advised to use effective contraception for 6 to 12 months following the treatment. Routine follow-up, including thyroglobulin and TSH level testing, and radioactive iodine diagnostic whole-body scans, is recommended for patients after thyroid cancer treatment. Efficient communication and collaboration among interprofessional healthcare team members are essential for achieving optimal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Radioactive Iodine Therapy for Thyroid Malignancies -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: An evidence-based integrated management approach brings superior results. The interprofessional healthcare team should educate patients about the risks and benefits of radioactive iodine treatment and inform them of the importance of following a low-iodine diet before therapy. Efficient clinician-patient communication and proper radiation safety instructions are essential. Small children or pregnant partners should not accompany the patient while visiting for treatment. Female patients of reproductive age should have a negative serum pregnancy test result to proceed with radioactive iodine treatment. The patient should be aware of this therapy's possible short- and long-term adverse effects.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypothyroidism -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: A comprehensive workup for other differential diagnoses is recommended for unresolved symptoms in the presence of biochemical euthyroidism. Although there is a lack of strong evidence supporting the routine inclusion of triiodothyronine (T3) preparations with levothyroxine in the treatment of hypothyroidism, there might be a need to consider alternative combination T4 and T3 therapy in cases that do not respond to conventional T4 replacement. [34] [35] The FDA has approved treatment options, including armor or nature thyroid; however, clinicians should understand that these formulations may increase cardiac arrhythmia risk. Moreover, these formulations are not approved for pregnant patients due to their T3 component or for patients with thyroid cancer, where strict TSH goals are required.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Effects on the Fetus -- Clinical Significance -- Thyroid Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: Thyroid cancer is another common cancer commonly seen in women of the reproductive age group. Surgery is the primary treatment for well-differentiated thyroid cancers. However, it is prudent to postpone surgery during pregnancy until after delivery and keep a watchful follow-up using ultrasound imaging and thyroid function tests unless progression is evident. Thyroid malignancies are not generally treated using external beam radiotherapy. Instead, radioactive iodine (131-I) is often used to ablate residual thyroid tissue after surgery in non-pregnant cases. Radioactive 131-I is contraindicated during pregnancy, and the Nuclear Regulatory Commission recommends not to breastfeed while being treated with radioactive iodine. For relatively rare occurrences of poorly differentiated or anaplastic thyroid cancer during pregnancy, aggressive therapy is warranted with the patient informed decision-making and constant communication within the multi-disciplinary team.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Disease and Pregnancy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This course provides participants with knowledge about the pathophysiology, clinical presentation, and evidence-based management of thyroid disease in pregnancy, emphasizes the interpretation of laboratory findings within the unique physiological context of pregnancy, and discusses strategies for managing overt and subclinical thyroid dysfunction. The course underscores the value of interprofessional collaboration, where the combined expertise of endocrinologists, obstetricians, nurses, and other healthcare professionals enhances patient outcomes. This collaborative approach facilitates comprehensive care, ensures timely interventions, and supports maternal and fetal health through coordinated diagnosis, treatment, and ongoing monitoring efforts.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Disease and Pregnancy -- Treatment / Management -- Surgery
retrieved_docs.content: Thyroidectomy is rarely needed and only reserved for patients who cannot tolerate thioamides due to severe side effects or when euthyroidism cannot be achieved despite using large doses of thioamides. Surgery is also an option in patients who have a large goiter causing compression issues. When indicated, it should be performed during the second trimester.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Disease and Pregnancy -- Etiology -- Other Forms of Hyperthyroidism and Thyrotoxicosis
retrieved_docs.content: A few rare neoplastic causes result in hyperthyroidism in pregnancy. Struma ovarii is a type of ovarian teratoma that contains functional thyroid tissue. [2] [13] A TSH-producing pituitary adenoma is another rare benign pituitary gland tumor capable of producing TSH. In a patient with thyroid cancer, metastatic lesions may have some functionality and produce TSH. [13] Please see the Medical Oncology section below.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Disease and Pregnancy -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: A strategic approach is equally crucial, involving evidence-based strategies to optimize treatment plans and minimize adverse events. Ethical considerations must guide decision-making, ensuring informed consent and respecting patient autonomy in treatment choices. Each healthcare professional must know their responsibilities and contribute their unique expertise to the patient's care plan, fostering a multidisciplinary approach. Effective interprofessional communication is paramount, allowing seamless information exchange and collaborative decision-making among the team members. Care coordination plays a pivotal role in ensuring that the patient's journey from diagnosis to treatment and follow-up is well-managed, minimizing errors and enhancing patient safety. By embracing these principles of skill, strategy, ethics, responsibilities, interprofessional communication, and care coordination, healthcare professionals can deliver patient-centered care, ultimately improving patient outcomes and enhancing team performance in the management of thyroid disease in pregnant individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperthyroidism in Pregnancy -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: There are a few rare neoplastic causes of hyperthyroidism in pregnancy. Struma ovarii is a type of ovarian teratoma that contains functional thyroid tissue and can be a rare cause of hyperthyroidism in pregnancy. [1] [9] A TSH-producing pituitary adenoma is a rare benign tumor of the pituitary gland that is capable of producing TSH. [9] In a patient with thyroid cancer, metastatic lesions may have some functionality and produce TSH. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Disease and Pregnancy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy is accompanied by metabolic changes associated with the thyroid gland. Therefore, it is important to understand the underlying physiological alterations to care for those pregnant with thyroid disorders effectively. This activity focuses on the physiology and management of hyperthyroidism, hypothyroidism, and thyroid nodules in pregnancy.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Disease and Pregnancy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Differentiate between normal pregnancy-related thyroid adaptations and pathological thyroid dysfunction. Screen pregnant individuals effectively for thyroid disease using appropriate laboratory and clinical assessments. Implement evidence-based treatment plans for hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism tailored to gestational age and patient needs. Collaborate among an interprofessional healthcare team to effectively detect thyroid disease in pregnancy and subsequent monitoring. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Sodium Iodide I 131 -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Radioactive iodine is widely used in the treatment of hyperthyroidism and thyroid carcinoma. Radioiodine administration requires a specialized interprofessional team of highly trained healthcare providers, including nurses, physicians, radiotherapy-specialized pharmacists, and radiation safety. All providers involved in radioiodine administration should be aware of its indications, contraindications, and adverse effects. One contraindication all providers should be mindful of is pregnancy. Providers should collaborate and ensure proper education of women of childbearing age to prevent radioiodine administration if pregnant and/or breastfeeding due to potential teratogenic effects; health care providers should obtain a thorough sexual history and implement routine pregnancy testing in women of childbearing age.

retrieved_docs.content: After the first trimester, when most organogenesis is complete, patients are then transitioned to methimazole. This transition is necessary to decrease the likelihood of hepatotoxicity, which is a rare but severe adverse event associated with PTU. [40]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2661
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy represent a broad category of maternal health issues that include specific conditions such as الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة (drug dependence before birth), خلل وظيفة الدرقية في فترة الحمل (thyroid dysfunction during pregnancy), and فقر الدم في أثناء الحمل (anemia during pregnancy). Recognizing these sub‑conditions under the umbrella of other complications of pregnancy is important for comprehensive prenatal assessment, as each can affect fetal development and maternal outcomes. Effective screening and management of drug dependence, thyroid abnormalities, and anemia are essential components of obstetric care to reduce risks to both mother and child.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1607
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy is a broad category that includes several specific maternal health issues. It has the child condition قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة (light vomiting of pregnancy) and is classified as encompassing الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة (drug dependence before delivery), الحصبة الألمانية خلال فترة الحمل (German measles during pregnancy), and خلل وظيفة الدرقية في فترة الحمل (thyroid dysfunction in the gestational period). These various complications highlight the diverse risks that can affect pregnant individuals and underscore the importance of comprehensive prenatal assessment and appropriate management to protect both maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2281
retrieved_docs.content: Mild hyperemesis gravidarum, thyroid dysfunction during pregnancy, and gestational herpes are all specific maternal complications that fall under the broader, uncategorized group of pregnancy complications affecting the mother. Each of these conditions represents a distinct health issue that can arise during gestation and requires appropriate clinical attention despite not being classified within more specific subcategories of maternal pregnancy disorders. Recognizing them as part of the general category of unclassified maternal pregnancy complications helps clinicians consider a wider range of potential problems when evaluating and managing pregnant patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2809
retrieved_docs.content: Elective termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري) is clinically associated with several obstetric conditions, including gestational diabetes (داء السكري أثناء الحمل), threatened miscarriage (تهديد بالإجهاض), and iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الحديد). These associations highlight the importance of evaluating and managing metabolic, hematologic, and early‑pregnancy complications when counseling patients about elective pregnancy termination, ensuring comprehensive risk assessment and optimal maternal health outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1532
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy are uterine conditions that arise within the uterus and are clinically associated with optional termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري). This relationship underscores that these complications are localized to the uterine environment and may influence or be influenced by decisions regarding elective pregnancy termination, highlighting the importance of uterine health in the management of pregnancy‑related problems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2088
retrieved_docs.content: The category “مضاعفات الحمل التي تصيب الأم، غير مصنف في موضع آخر” includes several specific maternal pregnancy complications: “الحصبة الألمانية خلال فترة الحمل” (German measles during pregnancy), “خلل وظيفة الدرقية في فترة الحمل” (thyroid dysfunction during pregnancy), and “فقر الدم في أثناء الحمل” (anemia during pregnancy). In addition, the broader category “مضاعفات الحمل التي تصيب الأم” (pregnancy complications affecting the mother) is itself classified under the same unclassified group. This hierarchical structure indicates that both the general set of maternal complications and the particular conditions of German measles, thyroid dysfunction, and anemia in pregnancy are grouped together as pregnancy complications not placed elsewhere, aiding clinicians in comprehensive assessment and management of maternal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 786
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal pregnancy complications that are not classified elsewhere include several distinct conditions. Mild hyperemesis gravidarum (قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة) represents a less severe form of pregnancy‑related nausea and vomiting. German measles occurring during pregnancy (الحصبة الألمانية خلال فترة الحمل) poses a risk to both mother and fetus and requires careful monitoring. Thyroid dysfunction in the gestational period (خلل وظيفة الدرقية في فترة الحمل) can affect maternal metabolism and fetal development, necessitating endocrine evaluation and treatment. Gestational herpes infection (الهِربسُ الحَمْلِي) is another maternal complication that may lead to adverse outcomes if not managed appropriately. Finally, anemia during pregnancy (فقر الدم في أثناء الحمل) is a common hematologic disorder that can compromise maternal health and fetal growth. Together, these conditions constitute a category of pregnancy‑related maternal complications that are recognized but not placed within more specific classification groups, highlighting the need for individualized assessment and management in prenatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1749
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy are classified as الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة, الحصبة الألمانية خلال فترة الحمل, and خلل وظيفة الدرقية في فترة الحمل. These represent drug dependence before delivery, rubella infection (German measles) occurring during gestation, and thyroid dysfunction arising in the course of pregnancy, respectively. Recognizing these conditions as part of other complications of pregnancy underscores the importance of assessing substance use, preventing and managing rubella infection, and monitoring thyroid function to protect maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.content: is also a member of this SMQ, linking a uterine pathology to the same set of pregnancy‑related risk considerations. Together, these relationships illustrate that normal pregnancy and its various multiple‑gestation outcomes are systematically classified and linked to a unified risk‑assessment framework for obstetric complications, facilitating comprehensive patient monitoring and targeted clinical management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1836
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal pregnancy complications, not classified elsewhere (مضاعفات الحمل التي تصيب الأم، غير مصنف في موضع آخر) is the overarching category that encompasses several more specific conditions. Within this parent classification are the child entities thyroid dysfunction in pregnancy (خلل وظيفة الدرقية في فترة الحمل) and gestational herpes (الهِربسُ الحَمْلِي), indicating that both of these disorders are considered types of maternal pregnancy complications that do not fit into other specific categories. In addition, the broader group labeled maternal pregnancy complications (مضاعفات الحمل التي تصيب الأم) is itself a child of the same parent category, showing that the general classification of maternal complications and the particular conditions of thyroid dysfunction during pregnancy and gestational herpes are all organized under the umbrella of complications that are not otherwise classified. This hierarchical arrangement clarifies how these specific and general maternal health issues are related for medical taxonomy and patient‑care planning.
